identity.title: Rope, Cordage, Twine, Tire Cord, and Tire Fabric Mills

lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers selling rope, cordage, twine, tire cord, tire fabric, and closely related reinforcing products repeatedly to external commercial, industrial, transportation, marine, agricultural, or distribution customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive manufacturing units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, firms outside the supplied EBITDA band, yarn and filament mills classified elsewhere, and operations without a transferable production and customer-service organization.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Predominantly repeat B2B product sales under purchase orders, distributor relationships, specifications, and periodic supply arrangements; revenue is tied to physical volume, mix, and negotiated unit prices rather than hourly billing.

a.bridge: BLS measures the employment and wage structure of four-digit NAICS 314900, not task-level AI exposure at 314994. I mapped high-exposure clerical, customer-service, purchasing, planning, sales-support, design, inspection, and supervisory documentation tasks to partial wage exposure, while assigning little substitution to machine tending, maintenance, and material movement. This likely overstates exposure if 314994 is more production-intensive than the broader group and understates it if machine vision and process-control AI mature quickly.
a.rationale: The closest published BLS mix is dominated by production occupations (61.92% of employment), while office and administrative support is 11.57%, sales 5.11%, business and financial operations 2.58%, and management 5.64%. AI can materially assist quoting, order entry, procurement, scheduling, quality review, documentation, and customer service, but most direct fabrication and handling work requires physical automation rather than AI substitution alone.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory from 10-15 US rope/tire-cord plants, split by occupation and current automation status, plus time-and-motion pilots for order-to-production, inspection, and planning workflows.
caveats: BLS publishes the relevant occupation mix at NAICS 314900 rather than 314994.
Employment shares are not wage shares; higher-paid management and commercial roles raise the wage-weighted exposure relative to headcount.
The estimate excludes tasks already automated and avoids treating all production-machine work as generative-AI substitutable.

rho.bridge: Auburn's 2024 smart-manufacturing survey covers 97 manufacturers across industries, while Zebra surveys manufacturing leaders globally; neither isolates small US rope and tire-fabric mills. I translate observed early AI adoption, implementation momentum, data-visibility gaps, and reported skills/business-case barriers into the share of exposed tasks operationalized within five years. The larger-company/global samples probably overstate readiness for LMM mills, while falling software costs may make them understate late-horizon adoption.
rho.rationale: Only 8% of Auburn respondents reported using AI, although the share implementing it rose by 12 percentage points; 58% remained at awareness or research and the leading barriers were lack of business cases and workforce skills. Zebra reports broad five-year intent but only 16% with end-to-end real-time production visibility. Five years is enough to implement bounded back-office, planning, and vision use cases, but legacy equipment, sparse data, validation, and change capacity limit full capture.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level interviews with LMM textile manufacturers on ERP/MES coverage, sensor history, AI pilots, implementation budgets, validation failures, and realized labor-hour reductions, supplemented by vendor deployment cohorts segmented by plant size.
caveats: The adoption surveys are not specific to NAICS 314994 and likely skew toward digitally engaged respondents.
Implementation is constrained more by data and workflow integration than by model capability alone.
The estimate concerns operational labor capture only, not pricing, demand, or valuation effects.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition describes manufacturers of physical rope, cordage, twine, and tire-reinforcement products. Most independent LMM firms in this population should sell repeatedly to external customers and therefore fit the lens, but captive units, highly project-based sellers, distressed asset shells, and firms lacking a transferable organization are excluded.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm census of the 49 dataset-estimated LMM candidates using ownership, customer mix, repeat-order share, captive status, and standalone management depth from Capital IQ, PitchBook, D&B, and owner interviews.
caveats: No public source measures lens eligibility within the supplied EBITDA band.
Tire-cord facilities may be more frequently captive or embedded in larger groups than rope and twine firms.
The injected firm count is an estimate and the eligibility share should not be read as a verified company count.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 62.4% of women owners of manufacturing employer businesses were age 55 or older in 2020; this is neither all owners, six-digit 314994, nor an observed transfer rate. I use it only as evidence of elevated succession exposure, then apply a bounded five-year realization judgment that many older owners will retain, close, transfer internally, or sell assets rather than complete a qualifying external control transfer. Women-owned manufacturing could be older than the full owner population, biasing the estimate upward, while the 2020 vintage and capital intensity may understate current succession pressure.
s5.rationale: Capital-intensive manufacturing has an older-owner profile, supporting meaningful succession-driven availability, but owner age is not transaction incidence. A base near one-quarter over five years allows for strategic sales and sponsor activity while excluding internal reorganizations, non-control investments, deaths without transferable operations, and closures.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed-control transactions and failed sale processes for US NAICS 314994 from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, BizBuySell, and broker records, matched to owner age, buyer type, and whether operations continued.
caveats: The source population is women owners across all manufacturing and is a broad proxy.
Age 55 or older spans very different retirement horizons and does not imply intent to sell.
Private-company transfers and closures are incompletely observed in public data.

q.rationale: Implemented savings first accrue to the manufacturer, but repeat B2B purchase orders and supply negotiations expose them to renewal repricing and competitive bids. Specification, qualification, reliability, custom products, and short lead times can preserve part of the benefit; commodity rope and tire inputs face stronger price comparison and customer sharing. The range discounts five years of pass-through rather than assuming permanent gross retention.
q.change_evidence: Customer- and SKU-level gross-margin bridges before and after labor-saving projects, including contract escalation clauses, bid frequency, renewal price changes, win/loss reasons, customer concentration, and competitor responses at 10 representative firms.
caveats: Public sources do not disclose contract-level pass-through for private NAICS 314994 firms.
Retention likely differs sharply between engineered tire reinforcement and commodity twine.
Raw-material volatility can obscure whether price changes reflect labor savings.

d5.bridge: Federal Reserve industrial-production data and BLS long-run output growth cover NAICS 314 textile product mills, not 314994 or a constant-quality rope/tire-cord service basket. I use the BLS 1987-2025 average real-output decline of 2.0% annually as the center, approximately 0.90 over five years, rounded to 0.91 in light of recent stabilization and industrial end-market breadth. The broad series may overstate decline if technical cordage gains from reshoring and safety-critical uses, or understate it if imports displace domestic commodity output.
d5.rationale: BLS reports textile product mill real output declining 2.0% annually over 1987-2025, while the Federal Reserve index rose from 82.98 in 2025 Q1 to 86.01 in 2026 Q1 after a long decline from its 2017 base. Rope, cordage, belting reinforcement, and tire fabric serve persistent industrial needs, but domestic volume faces import competition, substitution, and mature end markets.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year real shipment volumes for the specific Census product classes, separated into rope/twine and tire cord/fabric, with import penetration, customer-industry forecasts, plant closures, reshoring awards, and quality-adjusted price deflators.
caveats: The demand proxy is two digits broader than NAICS 314994 and mixes household and technical textile products.
Industrial production is domestic output, not underlying US consumption, so import-share changes can move it independently of demand.
The low and high cases reflect uncertainty across distinct rope, marine, agricultural, industrial-belting, and tire markets.

o.rationale: The current basket is physical, load-bearing or reinforcing product output that cannot be delivered by software alone. AI can change how mills quote, schedule, inspect, maintain, and document production, but accountable operators must still procure materials, run equipment, certify quality, fulfill orders, and bear product liability. A small residual allows for customer insourcing, product substitution, and fully automated capacity operated outside the lens.
o.change_evidence: Interviews with the top 20 customers and engineering specifiers on substitution, insourcing, qualification requirements, and willingness to buy from lights-out or platform-mediated suppliers, plus evidence from fully automated cordage plants.
caveats: Operator requirement is inferred from the physical production process rather than directly measured.
Imports can replace a domestic lens operator even though a physical operator remains somewhere in the supply chain.
The estimate is about quantity requiring an accountable operator, not domestic market share or employment intensity.

narrative.executive_view: This is a physical-manufacturing niche with repeat industrial demand and a large operator-required residual, but AI addresses only a bounded slice of payroll. The practical opportunity is concentrated in commercial administration, production planning, procurement, quality documentation, inspection support, and maintenance analytics rather than replacing the dominant machine-operating workforce.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate quoting and specification review, automate order and invoice handling, improve schedule and inventory decisions, support machine-vision inspection, summarize quality records, and assist predictive maintenance. Legacy machines, thin labeled datasets, safety and quality requirements, and limited implementation staff constrain how much of that potential becomes labor avoidance within five years.
narrative.value_capture: Savings initially improve manufacturing cost, yet periodic purchase orders, contract renewals, distributor negotiations, and competing suppliers create pass-through pressure. Retention should be better where qualification, custom construction, delivery reliability, or technical support matter and worse in readily comparable commodity products.
narrative.firm_availability: Most independent LMM mills should fit the repeat external-customer lens, although tire-cord assets may sit inside larger groups and some candidates may lack standalone management. Broad Census evidence points to older ownership in capital-intensive manufacturing, but actual qualifying transfers in this six-digit niche are not publicly measured.
narrative.demand_durability: Rope, cordage, twine, tire reinforcement, and industrial belting remain physical inputs, so software does not eliminate the underlying basket. Long-run US textile-product-mill output has declined, making a modest five-year contraction the central case despite recent stabilization and continued industrial applications.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The strongest risks are import displacement, customer concentration, raw-material volatility, capex-heavy modernization, product-liability obligations, and a mismatch between broad-industry AI surveys and small legacy plants. Evidence is particularly weak on six-digit transfer incidence, contract-level sharing of savings, and the task mix inside rope versus tire-cord facilities.
narrative.principal_driver: Persistent physical industrial demand keeps nearly all year-five quantity dependent on an accountable manufacturer.
narrative.principal_weakness: A production-heavy workforce and limited digital maturity cap the five-year implementable AI labor opportunity.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 314900
sources.supports: Closest published occupation mix: 59,910 jobs; production 61.92%, office/administrative support 11.57%, sales 5.11%, business/financial operations 2.58%, and management 5.64%, with detailed customer-service, planning, inspection, and textile-machine roles.

sources.title: ICAMS Smart Manufacturing Adoption Study 2024
sources.supports: Among 97 manufacturing respondents, 8% were using AI; the implementing share increased 12 percentage points, 58% were at awareness/research, and leading AI barriers included lack of business case and workforce skills.

sources.title: Zebra 2024 Manufacturing Vision Study
sources.supports: Reports 61% of manufacturers expected AI to drive growth by 2029, 92% prioritized digital transformation, but only 16% had real-time work-in-progress visibility across the full process and 86% struggled with technology pace and integration.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 314994 Rope, Cordage, Twine, Tire Cord, and Tire Fabric Mills
sources.supports: Defines establishments as manufacturing rope, cable, cordage, twine, and related products or cord and fabric used to reinforce tires, industrial belting, and similar uses.

sources.title: The Metamorphosis of Women Business Owners: A Focus on Age
sources.supports: Using 2021 Annual Business Survey data for 2020, reports that 62.4% of women owners of manufacturing employer businesses were age 55 or older, a broad succession proxy.

sources.title: Industrial Production: Textile Product Mills (NAICS 314)
sources.supports: Federal Reserve real-output proxy for domestic textile product mills: 82.9809 in 2025 Q1 and 86.0145 in 2026 Q1, index 2017=100, seasonally adjusted.

sources.title: Long-term percent change, hours and output, and employment
sources.supports: BLS reports textile product mills' real output declined at an average annual rate of 2.0% over 1987-2025 while hours declined 2.3%.

sources.title: SelectUSA Textiles Industry
sources.supports: Reports $23.9 billion of textile-product-mill shipments in 2023, about 95,000 employees in mid-2024, 5,206 establishments in 2022, and continuing industrial applications including tire-related technical textiles.
